speaker: interviewee
text: After this, I was-- well, when he got older, I was looking for a job to help out. And I was working for [INAUDIBLE] on Wilshire Boulevard as a salesperson. And then I worked for [? Bennish ?] Bakery for 10 years to help out, to put him through school. And life got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you missed-- you forgot to tell us you had a second child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Mark was born here in Los Angeles. Because after I was so sick, I was scared to have another baby. I went here to a doctor, and I told him the whole story. And he took wonderful care of me. And he was born in 1955. And I didn't have any problems after him. So everything was OK. And I stayed home until they were old enough to go back to work. They both went to public school. And they went to Hebrew school. So they have a Jewish education.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you don't mind, I'd like to just go back a little bit. I wanted to know if you could tell me if you know what happened to your family, and how you found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found out that they went from the ghetto to Auschwitz. My older brother was in the same camp that my cousins were. And he was just shipped out the week before from this camp to Auschwitz. If he would have been a week longer and my cousins came there, maybe he would've been alive. And I know my two younger brothers went with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know they were all--

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother studied to be like an accountant. My younger brother during the war was working for a dentist. And the youngest was still too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you-- did anyone-- has anyone ever-- has anyone been able to give you information as to when--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. But that happened after they were rounded up for that, they left everybody together in the ghetto. And from there, they were shipped to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's all the information you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about any of your grandparents? Where they alive when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Same time, same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My whole family was wiped out, except I have the two cousins here. We are the only three left from our family. From my father's side, I have an uncle in Israel that he came to Israel in 1939. And I have there two sisters and brother that live in New York. One lives here. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sons? What kind of families do they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional, like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, do they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My oldest son has a son. He's going to be 17 April 1st, next year. My granddaughter is going to be 16 December 29th. I have a granddaughter, 11, and a granddaughter who's going to be five in October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest son from San Francisco. And my youngest son lives in Rancho Bernardo. And my oldest son is an attorney. My daughter-in-law is an attorney. My youngest son is a doctor, and his wife is a teacher. And they have three little boys-- 8, 4 1/2, and a baby almost 10 months, three boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are there any messages, or are there any lessons that you've learned that you'd like to pass on to your children and grandchildren since the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my children know all about it. We were talking about it. They saw films. And the first time then they showed on television many years ago. And they happened to see it. I didn't even know. They called me home right away, was around 11:00 at night. And they said, we very sorry what happened, and we love you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had never really talked about this with your children prior to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they knew about it, but they never seen films. And my two older grandchildren seen now And my three grandchildren in San Francisco also called up on the phone. They had to write an essay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they talked to my husband and then me and then again. And they got As on their essays [INAUDIBLE]. And my son's asked me many times to put on tape things. But we just didn't do it. So what is it that you'd like to pass on to your children and your grandchildren about your life and what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went through a lot. And we came to the United States, which we're very grateful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For us, it's the best country in the world. And they should never forget what happened, and they should remember that they are Jews, and send the kids to Jewish schools and keep tradition, you know. And it should continue. Which, a matter of fact, they do send-- they had bar mitzvahs, bat mitzvahs, and the children are going to Hebrew schools also like they did, and they continue the tradition. And I'm very proud of the family, all of them. This is the picture from my parents, myself and my brother. I'm one year old and my brother's three years older. I really don't know-- I have quite a few pictures-- how I got them. [? I really don't know. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what year this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in '26, so must be '27.

speaker: interviewer
text: So '27. '27. And where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from a private kindergarten that was in a beautiful park taken with the three teachers. I have the names of it on the back on it. And I am sitting right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You need to be a little more descriptive.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the white blouse with the ribbon.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the front row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's on the front row on the right hand side, right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you're the second person from the right, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know approximately when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must've been four years old, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think this picture was taken in about 1930?

speaker: interviewee
text: I imagine so.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bedzin, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Bedzin, yes. It was a beautiful park, and we went there for all things in this park.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture-- I took an excursion with the school class from Bedzin to Krakow. And we went into the salt mine. And I bought a postcard to send home to my parents. And everything what you see on this picture, all the figures are made out of salt. It was just beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us when this might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must've been-- I don't know, when I was probably-- probably 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was close to the time of the war? Was this close to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was much before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you more like eight or nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, must have been that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two years before the war even, maybe, like 1937 do you think this picture might have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or three years before, probably, three years before the war. This is the postcard that I sent them from Krakow. And it's-- I wrote it to my parents, and this is my father's name, [? Moishe ?] [? Fischel ?] Bedzin, [INAUDIBLE] 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say up at the top?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- that was my name, Fischel Fela Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Moishe. [INAUDIBLE] Moishe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [? [INAUDIBLE]. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Maybe you can read it. Can't see so well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- what were you telling your parents on this postcard?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm having a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us about the postmark on the stamp, what the date is? Five [INAUDIBLE], it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 16th? Ninth? What is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: May. May '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, May '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. And this is a picture from the Polish [INAUDIBLE]. It's like a passport picture, because he was traveling a lot to close the Russian border. And that's original picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe Fischel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this picture was taken? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No? OK, let's move to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know when any--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Sarah. Sarah Goodman was her maiden name, Goodman, but then Fischel. At least my mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: And these pictures were all taken in Bedzin [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin. Those pictures are taken in Bedzin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's my brother. His name was [? Yankush. ?] And I think all those four pictures of the four of us were taken just the beginning of the war, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he your oldest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was two years older than me. And that's me. I was two years younger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My name was Felicia, but I changed it in the United States to make everything short. So mine is Fela.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my brother [? Shemek, ?] two years younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one right here, on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's the other one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other one is Kalma. And he was two years younger. We were all two years apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you explain, he was two years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years younger than [? Shemek. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And these were all taken when, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the beginning of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were these all taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all taken at Bedzin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture from my grandmother. Her name was Kayla. And my Uncle Joe, he lives in New York. He's 72 now. And I apparently had this picture. And I gave it to my uncle in New York, and he made a big portrait out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather died during-- after Hitler came in. That I remember. My grandmother perished in Auschwitz too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These are three friends that we were together in camp, and we were in the same room for 2 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken after the war, after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Munich, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember where we took that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Munich, 1945, it was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Munich, 1945? And these women-- are these women-- what was the name of the camp you were in with these women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schatzlar by [? Trautenau. ?] Schatzlar was a small camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A satellite of [? Theresienstadt, ?] correct? And they all survived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they all survived. The middle one I met in Israel. And the one on the right hand side, I know that she lives in France. And the one on the left side, I met her in New York many years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These were two girls that we were like the kindergarten of the camp. And one I met in New York, and one I met in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, do you know when these were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same time, must've been.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me some names? Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, unless it's written on the other side. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Sasha and Tushka were together with me in the same room for 2 and 1/2 years, and we were the kindergarten of the camp. On the right hand side, her name is Sasha. On the left side, her name is Tushka. And Sasha lives in Israel, and Tushka lives in Israel. I met them in Israel once.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us where and when you think these were-- this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken right after liberation, in Munich, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a Czech woman at the same town that I was in concentration camp. And she was so good to me. Apparently she liked me very much, and I was crocheting slippers, all kinds of things. And she used to bring a sandwich to me every day. And she was hiding the sandwich under this machine so nobody will see it. And from the beginning, she used to tell me also the news, what's going on between the Germans and the Russians. And later on, it had to stop because she has been watched. And that was a gift from her to me, right after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where-- where, though? Where, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture, she gave me in Schatzlar. That's the town that I was in camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year do you think this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was liberated, '45. I was liberated on May the 8th, 1945. And that was a week afterwards, she gave me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unless it's written on the back, I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her name is Kruka, and it was taken May--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fifth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fifth, 1945. And it was in the woods. And she used to work in the same [? spinnerei ?] factory that we were working. And so she lived close by to the factory where she was working. So I used to see her every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was near [? Theresienstadt. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Stuttgart. The picture was taken in the school, the [INAUDIBLE] that I used to go every day. And I wanted to learn a trade before I came to the United States, so I know something. So we were learning how to sew. And that's the class of all the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came '49, so must've been end of '47, beginning of '48 probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the center.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right in the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're right in the center behind the woman with the blond hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the woman with  all the big hair in the front of the picture, you're directly behind her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Breslau. That was right after liberation when I came to my cousins. And this is in the backyard of the building that we used to live. These are my two cousins, Abe and Jack [INAUDIBLE]. I'm at the center. And we are the only three survivors left from the whole family, which was wiped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, yeah, after liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, this is a picture. We arrived to Lancaster, Pennsylvania one week after we arrived to the city. That's me, my husband, and my cousin, Abe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us which one's-- who's who.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin's on the right hand side. I'm in the center. My husband is Nate on the left hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one month in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One week.

speaker: interviewer
text: One week in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: One week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- we arrived March 29, was a week later.

speaker: interviewer
text: April 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture, 1992, taken at the Friars Club. I was honored at that time from the B'nai B'rith [? Sheba ?] chapter. And I was president for two years, and the following year they honored me as sweetheart of the year. It was a beautiful party, and especially because my sons and my daughter-in-law were there. And I'm very proud of my sons and my daughter-in-law and the entire family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was-- and you said this was at the Friars Club.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on [INAUDIBLE] Santa Monica, in Beverly Hills.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had at that time 179 people attending the affair. And there was a lot of donations, of course. It went for a good cause.

speaker: interviewer
text: They have three children, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four. You have the little picture over there-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. That's not it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my son, Jack, my daughter-in-law Judy. They live in San Francisco. They're both attorneys. And my grandson, which is going to be 17 April 1st, and he's applying for pre-med school.

speaker: interviewer
text: My granddaughter Debbie-- did I say his name is Danny? My grandson's name is Danny. My granddaughter Debbie, she's going to be 16 December 29. And my granddaughter [? Laurie, ?] she's 11 and 1/2. And they all straight A students. And there's another girl there. And this picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Berkeley at the bat mitzvah of Debbie's-- at Debbie's bat mitzvah. And she was carrying on the whole service from the beginning to end. She was like a [INAUDIBLE] that time. It was just beautiful. And they very much involved with the temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years ago, so 1992.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago, yeah. This is my youngest granddaughter in Berkeley, and her name is Amy. And she's five years old. And my son and daughter-in-law, they have four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken when, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- must've been a year and a half ago, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think this was 1992? OK, this was at the same bat mitzvah, at the same time in Berkeley. And that's my daughter-in-law Felicia. And this is the older grandson is Aaron. He's eight years old now. And this is our second grandson. His name is Alex. He's four and a half. Is he-- Alex is being held by his mother, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one your husband is holding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and my husband and me. That's at the same bat mitzvah two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture's from 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, this is the grandchildren from my youngest son Mark, my daughter-in-law Felicia. The oldest son is Aaron. He's eight now. The second one is Alex. He's 4 and 1/2. And a new addition to the family. His name is Ethan, and he is now almost 10 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the oldest one is the boy on--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side. On the right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Rancho Bernardo, where they live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This year, six months ago, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
